Maladaptive Strategies.value: I drift off into a world of my own
I pretend it happened to someone else

Threat.value: Being superalert or watchful or on guard
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: I can't seem to put the frightening event into words.
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.

Negative Appraisals.value: I have permanently changed for the worse
I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
My reactions since the event mean that I am going crazy

vignette: Meet Jack, a 34-year-old man from Oceania who has been struggling to come to terms with his past. As a military veteran, he was exposed to traumatic events that have left an indelible mark on his life. The sound of uncomprehensible chatter or the presence of police can trigger a cascade of emotions and memories that feel overwhelming. In those moments, Jack often feels like he has no options, trapped in a world that is spiraling out of control. When these triggers occur, Jack's mind begins to wander, and he drifts off into a world of his own. He might dissociate from the present moment, pretending that the traumatic event happened to someone else. This coping mechanism provides temporary relief but ultimately reinforces his sense of disconnection from reality. As a result, Jack finds it challenging to articulate his thoughts and feelings about the traumatic experience, often struggling to put his emotions into words. Jack's symptoms are not limited to these triggers; they permeate every aspect of his life. He is constantly on edge, hypervigilant and watchful, as if waiting for the next threat to emerge. This state of hyperarousal makes it difficult for him to sleep, leaving him exhausted and irritable. His relationships suffer as a result, with his partner bearing the brunt of his anger outbursts. Jack's memories of the traumatic event are vivid and intrusive, often feeling like they are happening in real-time. Jack's perceptions of himself have become increasingly distorted since the trauma. He believes that he has permanently changed for the worse, losing the happiness and sense of self that once defined him. This fear of having "gone crazy" is a constant companion, one that fuels his anxiety and drives his maladaptive coping strategies. As Jack navigates this complex web of emotions and memories, it becomes clear that his experiences are deeply intertwined, with each aspect influencing and reinforcing the others. Despite these challenges, Jack remains in a relationship, although it is strained by his symptoms. His partner tries to be supportive, but Jack's withdrawal and irritability have created tension. As he struggles to reconcile his past and present selves, Jack is caught between a desire for connection and a need for escape. His world has become a fragile balancing act, with each trigger threatening to upset the delicate equilibrium that keeps him functioning.
